title: Single vs Dual Sourcing: Managing Supply Risk
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A practical UK QP guide to dual sourcing pharma vs single sourcing: use ICH Q9 to weigh supply risk, qualification cost and patient safety.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette over deep navy, with a single subtle green accent. Two parallel runs of identical sealed glass vials on a polished laboratory bench recede into a softly blurred background, evoking primary and backup supply lines; a gloved hand rests lightly near one row. Soft natural light rakes across the scene from the left, catching condensation on the glass and the brushed-steel surface. Shallow depth of field keeps the foreground vials crisp while the second line dissolves into bokeh, suggesting redundancy and resilience. Clean, premium, editorial composition, 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos.

faqs.q: Is dual sourcing a regulatory requirement in the UK or EU?
faqs.a: There is no blanket rule mandating dual sourcing for every material. However, EU GMP and ICH Q10 hold the manufacturer accountable for continuity of supply and the quality of outsourced activities, and ICH Q9 expects you to manage supply risk proportionately. For critical, sole-source materials, regulators and customers increasingly expect either a qualified second source or robust, documented mitigations.

faqs.q: How much safety stock should we hold for a single-source critical material?
faqs.a: Size it to the realistic recovery time, not a generic number of weeks. That means the supplier's lead time plus the time to re-qualify or activate an alternative, with a margin for verification and release. For a sole-source sterile component this can run to several months, and the calculation should be documented as part of your business continuity plan.

faqs.q: What is the biggest qualification pitfall when adding a second supplier?
faqs.a: Assuming the two sources are interchangeable without proving it. Differences in particle size, impurity profile, residual solvents or microbial control can affect your product, so comparability and, where relevant, stability bridging studies are essential. You must also confirm whether the change needs a regulatory variation with the MHRA before splitting or switching volumes.
